item.content:encoded: its… nice I think I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One grammatical pet peeve. 'Forensic' means that it is related to court processes and does not necessarily have anything to do with investigative processes. A better word to use in this context might be 'reconstructive.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels like the start of a great scp with many chilling field mission transcripts. I agree with most eveeyone that the basic concept is great, but you could add so much more. I feel as if I've been taken to a fancy restraunt, but have only been given bread sticks.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
The 1st image in this article is a CC BY-SA image from http://www.geograph.org.uk/photo/1527320.


This automated message was sent on behalf of apoplexic. Please do not reply to this message or send other messages to this bot. Contact apoplexic in regard to this message. If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was delivered incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was meh and no vote but then ALSO A SHARK downvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rather like it but I would do away with the incident log. It just doesn't feel like it belongs with the rest of the article and it removes a bit on of the mystery in my opinion. Plus I find the visual of a shark, no matter how big, ripping a boat to shreds rather silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
By definition, radar is a form of measurement using sound.

BZZZT! Nope, you're wrong. Radar was originally an acronym standing for "RAdio Detection And Ranging" and uses radio waves and microwaves to do its dirty work. So yep, speed of light.

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radar

Edit: I think you're thinking of "sonar", which actually is sound-based.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Radar doesn't move at the speed of light.
By definition, radar is a form of measurement using sound.

Regardless, you wouldn't be able to watch the changes in real time. Maybe Foundation scientists took an extremely high framerate video of it happening and saw it that way. (I honestly have no clue at all whether that last thing is even possible, cameras can pick up really fast frame rates but that seems like WHOA)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminded me of S.T.A.L.K.E.R. blowouts. Ultimately not too different from the concept, though significantly less deadly.

I want to upvote, and I want to downvote, so I'm abstaining from voting. I like this, but I'm irked by several things. First and foremost, the installation seems spooky and mysterious with various oddities for no other reason than to simply be odd. For example, one half of the island is missing via shearing? The rest of the effects listed by the anomaly don't coincide with the disappearance.

There seems to be a story behind this that we're not seeing, and I'd like to get more insight into the anomaly if at all possible - even if just barely enough to speculate, and attempt to piece together what happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What kind of topography changes? What kind of chemistry/organic content changes? Incremental alterations of previous state, or clean-slate redo-from-start type?

No upvote because insufficient detail. No downvote because failure to suck plus giant shark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We've got to get SPC on this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice setting to this one, and the megalodon was enough to get me to upvote.

I would ask, just what sorts of changes occur to the seabed, flora and fauna? Specifically speaking to the latter two, are they mutated, multiplied, swapped out for different species entirely? This point could definitely use a little expansion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some reason this reminded of this:

http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/prelude-the-sensation-of-falling

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
During each emission event, localized shifts in the area surrounding SCP-1210-2 occur that coincides with the propagation of the emission signal.

Unless one of the anomalous features of the radar signal is that it travels slowly, no one would be able to observe the shifts propagating, since radar travels at the speed of light, so to observers it would look like an instantaneous change across the entire region.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Someone better go out and get that megalodon punched.
Bagsy not me.

Kinda neutral on this, I like the whole 'changing local spacetime' hook, but it feels like there's not enough of it. Scarcity of information is good sometimes, but it feels like it should have an incident log or something just to keep it going.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It feels like SCP-084 but worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is kinda, meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been obsessing over Call of Pripyat recently and feels like something out of that game, so upvote!
However, it feels like something out of a game; not entirely sure if it is intentional or not, it is very good if it the relation is unintended and not as good if the connection was intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While reading through the first part of the article, my feelings were decidedly 'whatever.' Once I got to the addendum, I thought 'okay, I like this now.' Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I assume the radar is "resetting" the area a bit, altering aspects of the zone to the way they were in the prehistoric past. It seems to be copying chunks of space from that specific time frame into the present day. The SCP might paste a chunk of sky over where a person had been (effectively making them vanish), or paste a prehistoric shark into the water. Does the radar then just pull/replace full lifeforms, or can it chop them up by grabbing an incomplete segment? It seems to be chopping up landscape and buildings without making distinctions..so it might end up doing the same for lifeforms. If that's the case, then the island is going to start claiming some body parts (prehistoric and present-day). Also, if airborne creatures started showing up, it would be a noticeable breach if a full one ever showed up and escaped.

Also, the global sea level back then was about 100 meters lower than it is today. When sections of the sea that are at most 100 meters deep are replaced, they would be replaced with air/land. That probably causes some nasty turbulence/whirlpools in the ocean. I don't know if it would be necessary to put in the article, but I did want to bring it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote from me.
While I like the general feel of the article, and a vauge promise that 'there is more to it', I just don't find it all that interesting. There is a hook, but the bait's just not meaty enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotta say, I see potential in this one. +1 pending improvements. If it doesnt get better ill probably downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to second what VAE said here. Nondescript waves are one of my pet peeves, and until that's fixed, I'm downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a wave of pure time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A wave of what?
The information might well be redacted, but saying an anomalous wave holds little information - all that it says is that it is something periodic.
As such , I don't think it would appear in a scientific report. Same with "disappeared and were never found" - more like "are classified as MIA"

That said, i'm slightly meh about the object itself.

EDIT: And downvote removed upon fixing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Before someone ambushes me about my taxonomy…

The taxonomic assignment of C. megalodon has been debated for nearly a century, and is still under dispute with two major interpretations; Carcharodon megalodon (under family Lamnidae) or Carcharocles megalodon (under family Otodontidae). Consequently, the scientific name of this species has been commonly abbreviated to C. megalodon in literature.

Courtesy of Wikipedia.
